text: Welcome back to This Is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Bilal Bashir from HealthFirst.
Hey Bilal. Hey. Well, tell us a little bit about what HealthFirst does. HealthFirst is the largest
nonprofit health insurance plan in downstate New York City. We are proud to serve over 1.4
million members, predominantly in the Medicaid and Medicare space. That's great. So I love
your title. You know, you're the senior data wrangler, so you're swimming in data all day.
That's right. And one of the things that we like to do is revisit an architecture. And
you're going to come and tell us about how you've improved it since we last spoke to you.
But tell us a little bit about what you do with data for your members. So we utilize the
information from our claims information, our member information, and we utilize that to
make it better for our member. We're a digital, our digital strategy is enterprise first.
Fantastic. So run us through it. Sure. So it's a health insurance company. So as you
can imagine, there are a lot of near real time transactions coming in, different data sources.
We have roughly 20 plus data sources and about 40 terabytes of data that are coming in or
something's happening to them on a daily basis. So from these data sources, they go, they streamline
into this operations data database. And this is our operations environment that is handling
operational transactional purposes. So last time you were here, you talked about how you're
using serverless with data to do some processing. But I see now you're using some additional services
like CloudFormation. What's that for?
So we're using CloudFormation and SQS in hand. So everything kicks off and is deployed using
CloudFormation. So our ingestion acquisition pipelines, they get triggered from CloudFormation.
So either it's trigger-based, event-based, or time-based. And depending on that, CloudFormation will
either kick off a Redshift instance or it'll start Step Functions or it'll start Lambda or any other
service that we have.
So it's a great way to use the AWS services only when you need them. Reduces cost, which
is fantastic. But I also see you're using Step Functions now. Tell us a bit about that.
So last time we spoke, Step Functions was not available. Now it is available. And it fits in perfectly
with our event trigger-based architecture. We can do things like rules. We can do things like events
or timing. You can even embed if-then-else logics in there for different usage.
So as the number of event sources has increased, so have the rules. And it's a great way to get some
extensibility with your architecture while preserving all that business function in place, right?
Exactly.
Great. So the other thing I remember from the architecture, you had one place for all of your data using S3.
That's great. Tell us a bit about that.
Yeah. So our environment has definitely become more complex and we're more into the data management
space. With the help of our technology partners, we're able to create this analytics environment.
And each environment is an abstraction and it has a different purpose. So you have an analysis area, a core
strategic area, and an insights area. And all of these some way or somehow are to add value and support our
operations environment so that we can do some good in the world for our members. So you have, for example,
Redshift for this first environment. You'll have Step Functions for the second one for the core. And you have
either Lambda or Step Function. These can be interrelated. It doesn't really, it's not fit.
So S3 is awesome because, you know, it's a bottomless data store. What was the reason for the
segmentation?
The segmentation, it's to ensure and insist on data quality, data integrity. And there are
different aspects of that. So we wanted to ensure data consistency. So if any of these arrows here, any of
these pipelines fail, then we'll have SQS kind of jump in there for exception handling, for messaging, logging.
The other reason for having these different abstractions is to utilize like IAM service for more data protection.
So more isolation, obviously you're extending some of the event-driven capabilities of S3 to kick off those
different parts of the pipeline. That's right. Fantastic. So I see that you've also started using machine
learning since we last spoke to you. Tell us about how that is complementing everything.
Yeah. So now these different areas, you have your analysis area where we want to get data into the hands of
our consumers, our end users. That's one consumption mechanism for our core strategic efforts. We're using glue
throughout. So you have glue for ingestion, glue for transformation, and you also have glue for the load. And we're
using the catalog service as well, which has been exceptional. And then for this insights area, we're using machine
machine learning or AI related AWS services such as SageMaker, SageMaker Notebooks. And essentially what we're
doing over here is we're scoring all that data that we're receiving from the left side.
Right. So you're reusing a lot of the data that you're collecting, learning more from it, and it's adding to the scoring
capability of understanding your customers better. Exactly. So what we're doing over here, if you want to think about the first layer as kind of an R&D, and then from here, those creative insights or
creative thoughts start streamlining into some of our core business values, core business strategic initiatives. And then from there, once it gets tried and trusted, then we can bring it into this
insights environment, insights abstraction. And that's where our data science team, data engineering team can use some of these AWS services. And then we can bring it back into this
operational environment. And from there, that streamlines to other third parties, and we get data into hands of our company. And then we do good for our members.

That's fantastic. It's great to see this serverless data pipeline grow so big and using event driven, the event driven architecture, but also extending to use these purpose built data processing services from AWS.
Bilal, thank you so much for sharing your architecture with us today.
Thanks for having us. And thank you for joining us on This is My Architecture.
